title: GPU Memory Hierarchy
description: Master the GPU memory hierarchy: global, shared, register, and constant memory. Learn to use shared memory for inter-thread communication, synchronize with syncthreads, and optimize memory access patterns for coalescing.

sections.title: Why Memory Matters
sections.content:
Here is a fact that surprises most beginners: **the majority of GPU kernels are bottlenecked by memory, not computation.** A modern GPU like the NVIDIA A100 can perform 19.5 TFLOPS (trillion floating-point operations per second) but can only load data from global memory at 2 TB/s. A simple operation like vector addition does 1 FLOP per 12 bytes loaded (two reads + one write of 4-byte floats). At 2 TB/s bandwidth, you can load enough data for ~167 billion additions per second -- only 0.8% of the GPU's compute capacity. This means that **how you access memory determines your kernel's performance**, often more than what you compute. Understanding the GPU memory hierarchy is the single most important optimization skill in CUDA programming. Remember from Module 1 how we discussed the PCIe bottleneck between CPU and GPU? The memory hierarchy within the GPU itself has similar bottlenecks -- just at a smaller scale. And remember from Module 2 how each thread calculates its index and processes one element? Now we will learn HOW that data gets to the thread, and why some access patterns are 100x faster than others. ### The GPU Memory Hierarchy GPU memory is organized in a hierarchy, trading off size for speed. Think of it like a pyramid -- faster and smaller at the top, slower and larger at the bottom: ``` GPU Memory Hierarchy (Pyramid View) /\ / \ Registers (per thread) / R \ ~255 x 4 bytes, ~0 cycles / \ ~20 TB/s effective bandwidth /--------\ / Shared \ Shared Memory (per block) / Memory \ 48-164 KB, ~20-30 cycle latency / \ ~12 TB/s bandwidth /----------------\ / L2 Cache \ L2 Cache (all SMs share) / \ 6-40 MB, ~50 cycles / \ ~2 TB/s bandwidth /--------------------------\ | Global Memory | VRAM (all threads access) | (GDDR6/HBM) | 8-80 GB, 400-800 cycles | 320-2039 GB/s BW | The "slow" memory that +--------------------------+ holds all your arrays Speed: Registers >> Shared >> L2 >> Global Size: Registers << Shared << L2 << Global ``` Detailed architecture view showing how these fit inside the GPU: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Chip["GPU Chip"] direction TB subgraph SMs["Streaming Multiprocessors"] direction LR subgraph SM0["SM 0"] R0["Registers
~255 regs x 4B
~0 cyc"] Sh0["Shared Memory
48-164 KB
~20-30 cyc latency"] R0 --- Sh0 end subgraph SM1["SM 1"] R1["Registers"] Sh1["Shared Memory"] R1 --- Sh1 end subgraph SMN["SM N"] RN["Registers"] ShN["Shared Memory"] RN --- ShN end end L2["L2 Cache (shared across all SMs)
6-40 MB - ~50 cycles"] SM0 --> L2 SM1 --> L2 SMN --> L2 end GM["Global Memory (VRAM)
8-80 GB - 400-800 cycles - 320-2039 GB/s"] L2 --> GM classDef fast fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 classDef medium fill:#78350f,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#fef3c7 classDef slow fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca class R0,R1,RN,Sh0,Sh1,ShN fast class L2 medium class GM slow ```

sections.title: Registers & Shared Memory
sections.content:
### Memory Type Details

With the hierarchy in mind, let's look at each tier in turn -- starting with the fastest, smallest memory each thread owns privately, then moving outward to the on-chip scratchpad shared by an entire block.

#### 1. Registers (Fastest, Smallest, Per-Thread)

**What:** Each thread has its own private registers. Local variables in your kernel are stored in registers.

**Speed:** Essentially zero-cycle access (same speed as computation).

**Size:** Each SM has a register file of 65,536 32-bit registers, shared among all threads on that SM. Each thread can use up to 255 registers (the per-thread maximum). If your kernel uses 32 registers per thread and you want 1,024 threads per SM, you need 32,768 registers -- exactly half the file. More registers per thread means fewer threads can run concurrently (lower **occupancy**).

**Usage:** Automatic. When you write `x = a + b` in a kernel, `x`, `a`, and `b` are likely in registers.

```python
@cuda.jit
def kernel(data, output):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        x = data[idx]      # x is in a register
        y = x * x           # y is in a register
        z = y + x + 1.0     # z is in a register
        output[idx] = z     # write from register to global memory
```

**Pitfall: Register spilling and "local memory".** If your kernel uses too many local variables, the compiler runs out of registers and "spills" them to **local memory** -- which is, despite the misleading name, actually a per-thread region of slow global memory used as backing store for spilled registers. This silently kills performance.

To detect spilling, compile your kernel with verbose PTX output: `nvcc -Xptxas -v` (or look at numba's `inspect_asm` output). The compiler will report `N bytes stack frame, M bytes spill stores, K bytes spill loads` -- any non-zero spill numbers mean you are paying global memory cost on every spilled variable access. The fix is usually to split the kernel into smaller pieces or reduce the number of live variables.

#### 2. Shared Memory (Fast, Small, Per-Block)

**What:** A small, fast on-chip memory shared by all threads in a block. Think of it as a programmer-managed L1 cache.

**Speed:** ~20-30 cycle latency on Volta/Turing/Ampere, with ~1 cycle throughput per word when there are no bank conflicts. Roughly an order of magnitude faster than global memory.

**Size:** 48 KB to 164 KB per SM (depending on configuration and architecture). On the T4 GPU (Turing), it is up to 64 KB per SM, with up to 48 KB per block by default; programs can opt-in to larger per-block allocations via `cudaFuncSetAttribute` in CUDA C, but Numba kernels are limited to the default.

**Usage:** Explicitly declared and managed by the programmer using `cuda.shared.array()`.

```python
from numba import cuda, float32  # float32 must be imported from numba

@cuda.jit
def kernel_with_shared(data, output):
    # Allocate shared memory array -- visible to all threads in this block
    sdata = cuda.shared.array(shape=256, dtype=float32)

    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x

    # Load from slow global memory into fast shared memory
    if idx < data.size:
        sdata[tid] = data[idx]

    cuda.syncthreads()  # Wait for ALL threads to finish loading

    # Now work with data in shared memory (fast!)
    if idx < data.size:
        output[idx] = sdata[tid] * 2.0
```

> **Important:** The `dtype` argument to `cuda.shared.array()` must be a numba type like `float32`, imported from `numba`. Using `np.float32` will raise an error.

**When to use shared memory:**
- When multiple threads in a block need to read the same data (load once, use many times)
- When threads need to communicate within a block (e.g., reductions, scans)
- When you need to reorder data access patterns (e.g., matrix transpose)

sections.title: Global & Constant Memory
sections.content:
#### 3. Global Memory (Slowest, Largest, Accessible by All)

**What:** The GPU's main memory (VRAM). All arrays passed to kernels live here. This is the `cuda.to_device()` memory.

**Speed:** 400-800 cycles latency. The GPU hides this latency by switching between warps (this is why you need thousands of threads).

**Size:** 8-80 GB depending on the GPU. T4 has 16 GB.

**Bandwidth:** 320 GB/s (T4) to 2,039 GB/s (A100). This is high in absolute terms but much lower than shared memory.

**Usage:** Automatic -- all kernel array arguments are in global memory.

```python
@cuda.jit
def kernel(global_array):   # global_array is in global (device) memory
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    val = global_array[idx]  # ~400-800 cycle read from global memory
    global_array[idx] = val * 2  # ~400-800 cycle write to global memory
```

**Pinned memory and async transfer.** A frequently overlooked global-memory topic is host<->device transfer cost. By default, NumPy arrays live in pageable host memory, and the CUDA driver must first copy them to a pinned (page-locked) staging buffer before DMA'ing to the GPU -- effectively two copies. Allocating directly into pinned memory eliminates the staging copy and unlocks asynchronous transfers that overlap with kernel execution:

```python
import numpy as np
from numba import cuda

# Pinned host array (page-locked, faster H2D/D2H)
h_pinned = cuda.pinned_array(N, dtype=np.float32)
h_pinned[:] = np.random.randn(N)

stream = cuda.stream()
d_arr = cuda.to_device(h_pinned, stream=stream)  # async copy on stream
my_kernel[blocks, threads, stream](d_arr)         # runs concurrently with future copies
```

With multiple streams, you can overlap H2D copy of batch `n+1` with the kernel processing batch `n` and the D2H of batch `n-1`, hiding transfer latency entirely. This pattern matters for any pipeline that streams data through the GPU (deep learning training, video processing).

#### 4. Constant Memory (Cached, Broadcast, Read-Only)

**What:** A special region of global memory that is cached and optimized for the case where all threads read the same value simultaneously.

**Speed:** If all threads in a warp read the same address, one cache lookup serves all 32 threads (broadcast). If threads read different addresses, accesses are serialized.

**Size:** Limited to 64 KB total per kernel.

**Usage:** Best for lookup tables, configuration parameters, and convolution kernel weights -- anything small and read-only that all threads access uniformly.

Numba does expose constant memory directly via `cuda.const.array_like(arr)`, which copies a small NumPy array into the device's constant memory region:

```python
import numpy as np
from numba import cuda

FILTER = np.array([0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.2, 0.1], dtype=np.float32)

@cuda.jit
def convolve_1d(data, output):
    # 'filter' lives in constant memory; broadcast read across the warp.
    filt = cuda.const.array_like(FILTER)
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if 2 <= idx < data.size - 2:
        s = 0.0
        for k in range(5):
            s += data[idx + k - 2] * filt[k]
        output[idx] = s
```

When all 32 threads in a warp read `filt[k]` for the same `k`, the constant cache serves the whole warp from a single broadcast -- effectively free. This is exactly the pattern that makes constant memory shine for filter weights, lookup tables, and per-kernel constants. We will revisit this in Module 7 (Convolution).

#### Bonus: Read-Only Cache and Texture Memory

There is one more memory path worth knowing about: the **read-only data cache** (sometimes called the texture cache, since it shares hardware with the texture pipeline). It is a separate cache from L1, optimized for read-only data with spatial locality but possibly non-coalesced access patterns -- think gather operations, lookup tables larger than 64 KB, or random reads with reuse.

In CUDA C you opt in via the `__ldg()` intrinsic or by marking pointers `const __restrict__`. Numba's CUDA backend does not currently expose a direct `__ldg` builtin, but the JIT compiler will route loads through the read-only cache automatically when it can prove the array is not written within the kernel (i.e., when an array argument is only read). Practical takeaway: if you have a read-only lookup table that is too big for constant memory and has irregular access, just keep it as a normal global array -- the compiler will use the read-only path on its own.

### Memory Hierarchy Performance Summary

| Memory Type | Location | Scope | Size (T4) | Latency | Bandwidth | Managed By |
|-------------|----------|-------|-----------|---------|-----------|------------|
| Registers | On-chip | Per-thread | ~255 x 4B | ~0 cycles | N/A | Compiler |
| Shared | On-chip | Per-block | up to 64 KB per SM, 48 KB per block | ~20-30 cyc latency | ~12 TB/s (estimated) | Programmer |
| L2 Cache | On-chip | Global | 4 MB (T4); 40 MB on A100 | ~50 cycles | ~2 TB/s | Hardware |
| Global | Off-chip VRAM | All threads | 16 GB | 400-800 cycles | 320 GB/s | Programmer |
| Constant | Off-chip (cached) | All threads | 64 KB | ~5 cycles (hit) | Broadcast | Programmer |

> **Quick check:** Your kernel has a loop that reads the same global memory value 10 times. If you load it once into a register first, what is the rough cycle saving? (Answer: without the register, every iteration pays the global latency; with one load into a register, only the first iteration pays it. The compiler may already hoist the load if it can prove the value is invariant, but explicit `val = data[idx]` makes the intent obvious and is robust against optimization quirks.)

> **Gotcha:** Register spilling is silent and deadly. If your kernel uses too many local variables, the compiler spills some to "local memory" -- which is actually slow global memory despite the misleading name. Your kernel slows down dramatically and there is NO error message. Watch for kernels with many local variables that are inexplicably slow. On T4: a kernel with 40 local float32 variables will likely spill, going from ~0.5ms to ~3ms.

sections.title: Optimization Strategy
sections.content:
### The Fundamental Optimization Strategy Almost all GPU memory optimization reduces to one principle: **move frequently accessed data from slow memory to fast memory.** ```mermaid flowchart LR G["Global Memory
your arrays
400-800 cycles"] -->|"Step 1: Cooperative
tile load (all threads)"| S["Shared Memory
tile per block
~20-30 cyc"] S -->|"Step 2: Each thread
loads its values"| R["Registers
per thread
~0 cycles"] R -->|"Step 3: Compute
(free!)"| C["Result"] Note["Pay slow global cost ONCE,
reuse fast memory MANY times"] classDef slow fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca classDef fast fill:#365314,stroke:#84cc16,color:#ecfccb classDef fastest fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 class G slow class S fast class R,C fastest ``` 1. **Global -> Shared:** Load a tile of data from global memory into shared memory once. All threads in the block reuse it from shared memory (much faster access). 2. **Shared -> Registers:** Load values you use repeatedly into local variables (registers). 3. **Minimize global memory access:** Restructure your algorithm to maximize data reuse from fast memory. This tiling strategy is the core technique for high-performance GPU computing, and we will explore it deeply in the next section and in Module 4 (Tiled Matrix Multiplication). ### Think Like a GPU: Memory Edition Imagine you are one of 256 threads in a block, processing a stencil operation (averaging neighbors). Your CPU instinct says: "read my left neighbor, my value, and my right neighbor from the array." But think about it from the GPU's perspective. Thread 5 reads data[4], data[5], data[6]. Thread 6 reads data[5], data[6], data[7]. They are BOTH reading data[5] and data[6] from slow global memory! With 256 threads, most values are read 3 times from global memory. The GPU-thinking approach: ALL 256 threads cooperatively load their chunk into shared memory (1 global read each). Then each thread reads its 3 neighbors from fast shared memory. Result: 256 global reads instead of 768, plus shared memory is much faster for the neighbor reads. ### What Would Happen If... - **What if the GPU had no shared memory?** Every data reuse pattern would require going to global memory. Operations like matrix multiply, stencils, and convolutions would be 10-100x slower. Shared memory is what makes the GPU a programmable architecture rather than just a fixed-function throughput machine. - **What if you used ALL 65,536 registers on an SM for one block of 256 threads?** Each thread gets 256 registers -- plenty for complex kernels. BUT: only ONE block can run on the SM at a time, drastically reducing occupancy. With only 256 threads on an SM that supports 2,048, you have 12.5% occupancy, and the SM cannot hide memory latency effectively. - **What if you declared a shared memory array of 100 KB on a T4?** The T4 supports up to 64 KB of shared memory per SM (and 48 KB per block by default). Your kernel launch would fail with: ``` numba.cuda.cudadrv.driver.CudaAPIError: [1] Call to cuLaunchKernel results in CUDA_ERROR_INVALID_VALUE ```
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda
from numba import float32

# ============================================
# Demonstrating Memory Hierarchy Performance
# ============================================

# Kernel 1: Repeated reads from GLOBAL memory
@cuda.jit
def global_memory_kernel(data, output):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        # Read the same value from global memory 10 times
        total = 0.0
        for i in range(10):
            total += data[idx]  # Each read goes to global memory
        output[idx] = total

# Kernel 2: Load to register once, reuse from register
@cuda.jit
def register_kernel(data, output):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        val = data[idx]  # One global read -> register
        total = 0.0
        for i in range(10):
            total += val  # Reads from register (essentially free)
        output[idx] = total

# Kernel 3: Using shared memory for inter-thread data sharing
@cuda.jit
def shared_memory_kernel(data, output):
    sdata = cuda.shared.array(256, dtype=float32)
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x

    # Load into shared memory
    if idx < data.size:
        sdata[tid] = data[idx]
    cuda.syncthreads()

    # Each thread reads its neighbor's value from shared memory
    if idx < data.size and tid > 0:
        output[idx] = sdata[tid] + sdata[tid - 1]  # Fast: shared memory
    elif idx < data.size:
        output[idx] = sdata[tid]

# Setup
n = 10_000_000
data = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
d_data = cuda.to_device(data)
d_out = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)

threads = 256
blocks = (n + threads - 1) // threads

# Warm up all kernels
global_memory_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
register_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
shared_memory_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()

# Benchmark
iterations = 100

start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iterations):
    global_memory_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()
global_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iterations

start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iterations):
    register_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()
register_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iterations

start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iterations):
    shared_memory_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()
shared_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iterations

print("=== Memory Hierarchy Performance ===")
print(f"Global memory (10 reads): {global_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"Register (1 read, 10 reuse): {register_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"Shared memory (neighbor access): {shared_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"\nSpeedup (register vs global): {global_time/register_time:.2f}x")
print(f"Key insight: Loading to a register once and reusing is much")
print(f"faster than reading global memory repeatedly.")

# ============================================
# Query memory information
# ============================================
device = cuda.get_current_device()
print(f"\n=== GPU Memory Info ===")
print(f"Max shared memory per block: {device.MAX_SHARED_MEMORY_PER_BLOCK:,} bytes")
print(f"  = {device.MAX_SHARED_MEMORY_PER_BLOCK / 1024:.0f} KB")
print(f"Multiprocessor count: {device.MULTIPROCESSOR_COUNT}")
print(f"Warp size: {device.WARP_SIZE}")

# Show GPU memory usage
free, total = cuda.current_context().get_memory_info()
print(f"\nGPU VRAM: {total / 1e9:.1f} GB total, {free / 1e9:.1f} GB free")
print(f"  Used: {(total - free) / 1e6:.0f} MB")

sections.title: Shared Memory Basics
sections.content:
Shared memory is the GPU programmer's most powerful optimization tool. It is a small, fast, on-chip memory that all threads in a block can read and write. Think of it as a scratchpad that you, the programmer, control explicitly -- unlike CPU caches which are managed automatically by hardware.

In the previous section, we saw the memory hierarchy pyramid. Now we zoom in on the middle layer -- shared memory -- and learn how to use it in practice. This is where GPU programming gets interesting: you are no longer just writing "what to compute" but also "how to manage data movement."

### Declaring Shared Memory in Numba

In numba.cuda, shared memory is allocated inside the kernel using `cuda.shared.array()`:

```python
from numba import cuda, float32  # float32 type must come from numba

@cuda.jit
def my_kernel(data):
    # Allocate a shared memory array of 256 float32 elements
    # This array is shared by ALL threads in the block
    shared = cuda.shared.array(shape=256, dtype=float32)

    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x
    idx = cuda.grid(1)

    # Each thread loads one element from global to shared memory
    if idx < data.size:
        shared[tid] = data[idx]

    # CRITICAL: synchronize before reading shared data written by other threads
    cuda.syncthreads()

    # Now all threads can safely read any element in shared[]
    # ...
```

Key points:
- **`shape` must be a compile-time constant.** You cannot use a variable for the size. This is a limitation of GPU hardware -- shared memory is allocated when the block is scheduled, and the size must be known at compile time. In numba, you can use a module-level constant (e.g., `BLOCK_SIZE = 256`) but not a function parameter.
- **The array is shared by all threads in the block.** If thread 0 writes to `shared[0]`, thread 5 can read it (after synchronization).
- **Each block gets its own independent copy.** Block 0's shared memory is completely separate from Block 1's.

sections.title: Synchronization with syncthreads
sections.content:
### The Critical Importance of cuda.syncthreads()

`cuda.syncthreads()` is a **barrier synchronization** primitive. When a thread reaches `syncthreads()`, it stops and waits until **every thread in the block** reaches the same point. Only then do all threads proceed.

This is essential because threads in a block execute in warps, and warps may execute in any order. Without synchronization:

```python
# DANGEROUS: No synchronization
@cuda.jit
def buggy_kernel(data, output):
    shared = cuda.shared.array(256, dtype=float32)
    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x
    idx = cuda.grid(1)

    if idx < data.size:
        shared[tid] = data[idx]          # Thread 0 writes shared[0]

    # NO syncthreads() here!

    if idx < data.size and tid > 0:
        output[idx] = shared[tid - 1]    # Thread 1 reads shared[0]
        # But thread 0 might not have written yet! Race condition!
```

The fix:

```python
@cuda.jit
def correct_kernel(data, output):
    shared = cuda.shared.array(256, dtype=float32)
    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x
    idx = cuda.grid(1)

    if idx < data.size:
        shared[tid] = data[idx]

    cuda.syncthreads()  # ALL threads wait here until ALL have written

    if idx < data.size and tid > 0:
        output[idx] = shared[tid - 1]  # Now safe to read any shared element
```

> **Rule:** Whenever one thread reads data that another thread wrote to shared memory, there **must** be a `syncthreads()` between the write and the read. No exceptions.

> **Gotcha:** Forgetting `cuda.syncthreads()` after loading shared memory will cause race conditions that are INTERMITTENT -- your code might work 99% of the time and fail 1%. This is because the bug only manifests when warp scheduling happens to interleave reads and writes in the wrong order, which depends on runtime conditions. These are among the hardest bugs to find in GPU programming. Always add syncthreads defensively after shared memory writes.

#### syncthreads() vs threadfence(): When You Need a Memory Fence

`syncthreads()` is a barrier *within* a single block: it synchronizes execution and also flushes shared-memory writes so other threads in the block can see them. It does **nothing** across blocks. For the rare cases where you need ordering of global-memory writes across the entire device -- e.g., a producer block writing a result that a consumer block must observe -- you need a memory fence:

- `cuda.threadfence()` (numba) / `__threadfence()` (CUDA C): ensures that all global-memory writes the calling thread issued *before* the fence are visible to all threads in the device *before* any writes it issues *after*. It is an ordering guarantee, not a barrier -- other threads do not wait.
- `cuda.threadfence_block()`: same ordering, but only within the calling block.

Fences matter for inter-block communication patterns -- the classic example is the "last block sets a flag" pattern used in single-pass reductions (Module 8). Most kernels you write will never need a fence; if you do, it is usually a sign that you should split into two kernel launches instead, since cross-block synchronization is expensive and easy to get wrong.

### syncthreads() Pitfall: Conditional Branches

A subtle but deadly bug:

```python
# DEADLOCK: syncthreads inside a conditional that not all threads enter
@cuda.jit
def deadlock_kernel(data, output):
    shared = cuda.shared.array(256, dtype=float32)
    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x
    idx = cuda.grid(1)

    if idx < data.size:  # Some threads might skip this if idx >= data.size
        shared[tid] = data[idx]
        cuda.syncthreads()  # DEADLOCK: threads that skipped the if never reach this
        output[idx] = shared[tid]
```

The fix: move `syncthreads()` outside the conditional:

```python
@cuda.jit
def safe_kernel(data, output):
    shared = cuda.shared.array(256, dtype=float32)
    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x
    idx = cuda.grid(1)

    if idx < data.size:
        shared[tid] = data[idx]

    cuda.syncthreads()  # Outside the conditional: ALL threads participate

    if idx < data.size:
        output[idx] = shared[tid]
```

sections.title: Reduction Example
sections.content:
### Practical Example: Parallel Reduction (Sum)

A classic use of shared memory is **parallel reduction** -- computing the sum of a large array using a tree-based approach:

```python
@cuda.jit
def block_sum(data, block_sums):
    shared = cuda.shared.array(256, dtype=float32)
    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x
    idx = cuda.grid(1)

    # Load into shared memory
    shared[tid] = data[idx] if idx < data.size else 0.0
    cuda.syncthreads()

    # Tree reduction within the block
    stride = cuda.blockDim.x // 2  # Start at half the block size
    while stride > 0:
        if tid < stride:
            shared[tid] += shared[tid + stride]
        cuda.syncthreads()  # Sync after each level
        stride //= 2

    # Thread 0 of each block writes the block's sum
    if tid == 0:
        block_sums[cuda.blockIdx.x] = shared[0]
```

Visualized for 8 threads:

```
  Parallel Reduction: Tree-based Sum (8 threads)
  
  Step 0 (initial data):    [a0, a1, a2, a3, a4, a5, a6, a7]
  
  Step 1 (stride=4):         a0   a1   a2   a3
                             +    +    +    +
                             a4   a5   a6   a7
                             =    =    =    =
                            [s04, s15, s26, s37, -,  -,  -,  - ]
                            syncthreads()
  
  Step 2 (stride=2):         s04  s15
                             +    +
                             s26  s37
                             =    =
                            [s0246, s1357, -,  -,  -,  -,  -,  - ]
                            syncthreads()
  
  Step 3 (stride=1):         s0246
                             +
                             s1357
                             =
                            [TOTAL, -,  -,  -,  -,  -,  -,  - ]
  
  Only 3 steps (log2(8)) instead of 7 sequential additions!
  Thread 0 writes the final sum to block_sums[blockIdx.x]
```

Only 3 steps (log2(8)) instead of 7 sequential additions. For 1 million elements with blocks of 256, the first pass reduces to ~4,000 partial sums, and a second pass reduces to 1.

> **Quick check:** If you have 10 million elements and a block size of 256, how many blocks does the first pass produce? How many levels of reduction do you need? (Answer: ceil(10M/256) = 39,063 partial sums. Second pass: ceil(39,063/256) = 153 partial sums. Third pass: 1 sum. Three passes total.)

On T4 GPU, summing 10M float32 elements:
- NumPy `np.sum()`: ~2.5ms
- GPU two-pass reduction: ~0.4ms (including kernel launches)
- Note: The GPU sum may differ slightly from CPU due to different floating-point accumulation order
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda, float32

# ============================================
# Shared Memory: Parallel Reduction (Block Sum)
# ============================================

BLOCK_SIZE = 256

@cuda.jit
def block_reduce_sum(data, partial_sums):
    """Each block computes the sum of its elements using shared memory."""
    shared = cuda.shared.array(BLOCK_SIZE, dtype=float32)
    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x
    idx = cuda.grid(1)

    # Load into shared memory (0 for out-of-bounds threads)
    shared[tid] = data[idx] if idx < data.size else 0.0
    cuda.syncthreads()

    # Tree reduction
    stride = BLOCK_SIZE // 2
    while stride > 0:
        if tid < stride:
            shared[tid] += shared[tid + stride]
        cuda.syncthreads()
        stride //= 2

    # Thread 0 writes the block sum
    if tid == 0:
        partial_sums[cuda.blockIdx.x] = shared[0]

def gpu_sum(data):
    """Sum a large array using two-pass reduction."""
    n = data.size
    d_data = cuda.to_device(data)

    # Pass 1: reduce each block
    blocks = (n + BLOCK_SIZE - 1) // BLOCK_SIZE
    d_partial = cuda.device_array(blocks, dtype=np.float32)
    block_reduce_sum[blocks, BLOCK_SIZE](d_data, d_partial)

    # Pass 2: reduce the partial sums (if they fit in one block)
    if blocks <= BLOCK_SIZE:
        d_result = cuda.device_array(1, dtype=np.float32)
        block_reduce_sum[1, BLOCK_SIZE](d_partial, d_result)
        return d_result.copy_to_host()[0]
    else:
        # Recursively reduce
        return gpu_sum(d_partial.copy_to_host())

# Test reduction
print("=== Parallel Reduction (Sum) ===")
n = 1_000_000
data = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)

gpu_result = gpu_sum(data)
cpu_result = np.sum(data)
print(f"GPU sum: {gpu_result:.4f}")
print(f"CPU sum: {cpu_result:.4f}")
print(f"Difference: {abs(gpu_result - cpu_result):.6f}")
print(f"(Small difference is expected due to float32 accumulation order)")

# ============================================
# Shared Memory: Neighbor Averaging (Stencil)
# ============================================
print("\n=== Stencil Operation (3-point average) ===")

# WITHOUT shared memory: each element reads 3 values from global memory
@cuda.jit
def stencil_global(data, output):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx > 0 and idx < data.size - 1:
        output[idx] = (data[idx-1] + data[idx] + data[idx+1]) / 3.0

# WITH shared memory: load block into shared, read neighbors from there
@cuda.jit
def stencil_shared(data, output):
    shared = cuda.shared.array(BLOCK_SIZE + 2, dtype=float32)  # +2 for halo elements
    tid = cuda.threadIdx.x
    idx = cuda.grid(1)

    # Load main data
    if idx < data.size:
        shared[tid + 1] = data[idx]  # +1 offset for left halo

    # Load halo elements (boundary of the block)
    if tid == 0 and idx > 0:
        shared[0] = data[idx - 1]  # Left halo
    if tid == BLOCK_SIZE - 1 and idx < data.size - 1:
        shared[BLOCK_SIZE + 1] = data[idx + 1]  # Right halo

    cuda.syncthreads()

    # Compute stencil from shared memory
    if idx > 0 and idx < data.size - 1:
        output[idx] = (shared[tid] + shared[tid + 1] + shared[tid + 2]) / 3.0

n = 10_000_000
data = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
d_data = cuda.to_device(data)
d_out_global = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
d_out_shared = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)

blocks = (n + BLOCK_SIZE - 1) // BLOCK_SIZE

# Warm up
stencil_global[blocks, BLOCK_SIZE](d_data, d_out_global)
stencil_shared[blocks, BLOCK_SIZE](d_data, d_out_shared)
cuda.synchronize()

# Benchmark
iters = 100
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iters):
    stencil_global[blocks, BLOCK_SIZE](d_data, d_out_global)
cuda.synchronize()
global_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iters):
    stencil_shared[blocks, BLOCK_SIZE](d_data, d_out_shared)
cuda.synchronize()
shared_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

# Verify correctness
out_g = d_out_global.copy_to_host()
out_s = d_out_shared.copy_to_host()
assert np.allclose(out_g[1:-1], out_s[1:-1], atol=1e-5)

print(f"Global memory version: {global_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"Shared memory version: {shared_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"Speedup: {global_time/shared_time:.2f}x")
print(f"\nFor stencil ops, shared memory helps because each element")
print(f"is read by 3 threads. Loading to shared memory turns 3 global")
print(f"reads into 1 global read + 3 fast shared reads.")

sections.title: Bank Conflicts & When Not to Use
sections.content:
### Bank Conflicts: The Hidden Performance Killer Shared memory looks like a flat array, but underneath it is divided into **32 banks** -- one per warp lane -- each 4 bytes wide. Successive 4-byte words map to successive banks: word `i` lives in bank `i % 32`. Banks can serve one 4-byte access per cycle each, so 32 threads in a warp accessing 32 *different* banks all complete in a single cycle. When two or more threads in the same warp hit the *same* bank with *different* addresses, the hardware serializes those accesses -- this is a **bank conflict**, and an N-way conflict means N cycles where there should have been 1. **No conflict (ideal):** Each thread accesses a different bank. ```mermaid flowchart LR T0[T0] --> B0["Bank 0
shared[0]"] T1[T1] --> B1["Bank 1
shared[1]"] T2[T2] --> B2["Bank 2
shared[2]"] Te[...] --> Be[...] T31[T31] --> B31["Bank 31
shared[31]"] Note["No conflict: 1 cycle for all 32 threads"] classDef ok fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 class B0,B1,B2,B31,Be ok ``` All 32 accesses happen simultaneously in one cycle. **2-way bank conflict:** Two threads hit the same bank. ```mermaid flowchart LR T0["T0 -> shared[0]"] --> B0[Bank 0] T1["T1 -> shared[32]"] --> B0 T2["T2 -> shared[1]"] --> B1[Bank 1] T3["T3 -> shared[33]"] --> B1 T4["T4 -> shared[2]"] --> B2[Bank 2] T5["T5 -> shared[34]"] --> B2 Note["2-way conflict: 2 threads per bank
Result: 2 cycles (serialized)"] classDef bad fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca class B0,B1,B2 bad ``` Two cycles needed instead of one (2x slowdown). **How banks are assigned:** Bank number = (word index) % 32 = (byte address / 4) % 32. So `shared[0]` and `shared[32]` map to the same bank (bank 0). Stride-32 access -- e.g., `shared[tid * 32]` -- is the worst case: all 32 threads hit bank 0 and the warp is fully serialized into 32 cycles. **Broadcast exception:** If all threads in a warp read the **same** address, the hardware does a broadcast (one read serves all 32 threads, no conflict). Broadcast is always free, even though by the bank rule it would look like a 32-way conflict. **The +1 padding trick, explained.** A common shared-memory pattern is a 2D tile, e.g. `tile[32][32]` for a transpose. Reading a *row* (`tile[ty][tx]` with `tx` varying across the warp) hits 32 different banks -- no conflict. Reading a *column* (`tile[tx][ty]` with `tx` varying) hits address `tx*32 + ty`; for a fixed `ty`, every thread's address differs by exactly 32 words and thus lands in the same bank -- a 32-way conflict, fully serialized. The fix is to declare the tile one column wider: `tile[32][33]`. Now stepping down a column moves by 33 words -- coprime with 32 -- so every thread lands in a different bank. The extra column is wasted memory, but it converts a 32-way conflict (32 cycles) into no conflict (1 cycle), a 32x speedup for that access. You will see this exact `(TILE_DIM, TILE_DIM + 1)` pattern in the tiled transpose later in this module. ```python # BAD: 32-way bank conflict (stride-32 access) val = shared[tid * 32] # All threads hit bank 0! # GOOD: No bank conflict (consecutive access) val = shared[tid] # Thread 0 -> bank 0, thread 1 -> bank 1, etc. # GOOD: No conflict (stride-1 access with offset) val = shared[tid + offset] # Still consecutive banks ``` ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph nocon["NO CONFLICT (stride-1) - 1 cycle"] direction LR NA["T0->B0 | T1->B1 | T2->B2 | ... | T31->B31"] end subgraph two["2-WAY CONFLICT (stride-2) - 2 cycles"] direction LR NB["T0,T2->B0 | T1,T3->B1 | ... (each bank hit by 2 threads)"] end subgraph max["32-WAY CONFLICT (stride-32) - 32 cycles"] direction LR NC["T0,T1,...,T31 ALL -> Bank 0 (fully serialized)"] end classDef good fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 classDef warn fill:#78350f,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#fef3c7 classDef bad fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca class NA good class NB warn class NC bad ``` **How to detect bank conflicts.** Nsight Compute reports them directly via the `smsp__sass_l1tex_data_bank_conflicts_pipe_lsu_mem_shared_op_*` metrics (or the friendlier "Shared Memory" section of the Memory Workload Analysis). A non-zero conflict count on a hot kernel is your signal to pad shared arrays or rearrange access patterns. ### What Would Happen If... - **What if you forgot `cuda.syncthreads()` between the load and the reduction steps?** The reduction would read partially-loaded shared memory. Some threads might read zeros (or uninitialized values) instead of the data other threads were supposed to write. Results would be wrong -- but only sometimes, making this extremely hard to debug. - **What if you put `syncthreads()` inside the `if tid < stride:` block in the reduction?** Only threads with `tid < stride` would reach the barrier. The other threads would never call `syncthreads()`. Result: deadlock. The kernel hangs forever (until you kill it or the GPU watchdog timer fires, typically after ~5 seconds). ### When NOT to Use Shared Memory Shared memory is not always beneficial: 1. **If each data element is used only once,** shared memory adds overhead without benefit. Just read from global memory directly. This was the case for vector addition in Module 2 -- each element is read once, used once, no sharing needed. 2. **If threads do not need to communicate,** shared memory serves no purpose. Element-wise operations from Module 2 (sigmoid, ReLU) fall in this category. 3. **If your kernel is compute-bound** (not memory-bound), optimizing memory access will not help. 4. **If using shared memory reduces occupancy** (fewer blocks per SM due to shared memory limits), the net effect may be negative. > **Gotcha:** Adding shared memory to a kernel that does not need it can actually make it SLOWER. The shared memory allocation reduces the number of blocks that can run simultaneously on an SM, lowering occupancy. For simple element-wise kernels, the overhead of loading data into shared memory and synchronizing can exceed the benefit. Profile first, then optimize.

sections.title: What is Coalescing?
sections.content:
Memory coalescing is the GPU's ability to combine multiple memory accesses from threads in a warp into a single, efficient memory transaction. It is one of the most important performance concepts in CUDA programming, and getting it wrong can reduce your effective memory bandwidth by 32x.

Remember from the previous section how shared memory gives us fast per-block storage? Coalescing is about how we get data from slow global memory into that fast storage (or into registers) as efficiently as possible. Think of it as the "loading dock" -- the actual computation might be fast, but if you load data inefficiently, the whole pipeline stalls.

### How Global Memory Works

GPU global memory is accessed in **cache lines** of 128 bytes (32 float32 values). When any thread in a warp reads a single float32, the hardware fetches the entire 128-byte cache line containing that value. (Pedagogical simplification: the L1 cache line is 128 B, while the L2 transaction unit is a 32-byte sector. The big picture -- "you pay for the whole line, so use it" -- is the same.)

The best case: all 32 threads in a warp access 32 consecutive float32 values that fall in a single 128-byte cache line. One memory transaction serves all threads.

The worst case: all 32 threads access addresses in 32 different cache lines. Thirty-two memory transactions are needed, each fetching 128 bytes but using only 4 bytes. That is 4096 bytes fetched for 128 bytes of useful data -- **97% waste.**

### The Exact Coalescing Rule

The rule that the hardware actually applies, stated precisely:

1. **Per warp.** Coalescing is decided one warp at a time -- 32 threads. Every load or store the warp issues is partitioned into the minimum number of 128-byte (L1) or 32-byte (L2 sector) transactions that cover the addresses the warp touched.
2. **Alignment matters.** A 128-byte transaction is aligned to a 128-byte boundary. If a warp's 32 contiguous float32 reads start at an address that is *not* a 128-byte multiple, the warp's footprint straddles two segments and you pay for two transactions instead of one. Concretely: `data[0..31]` is 1 transaction; `data[1..32]` (offset by 4 bytes) is 2 transactions.
3. **Width matters.** The compiler may emit 4-, 8-, or 16-byte loads per thread (e.g., when accessing `float4`/`int4` or when the access pattern is provably vectorizable). A warp doing 32 x 16-byte loads needs 4 fully-utilized 128-byte transactions (512 bytes of useful data) -- still perfect efficiency, just at higher granularity.
4. **Partial transactions still cost a full segment.** If only one thread in a warp does a load, the hardware still fetches the full 128-byte (or 32-byte sector) line covering that address. There is no "just fetch the bytes I asked for" path.

**The 32 x 4 = 128 example.** The textbook coalesced load is 32 threads x 4 bytes = 128 bytes, perfectly aligned to a 128-byte boundary -- one transaction, 100% useful. Drop the alignment by even 1 element and you cross a segment, doubling cost. This is why the start of every flat array `cuda.to_device()` returns is aligned (the driver guarantees it), and why the `idx = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x` indexing pattern from Module 2 is the natural coalesced shape.

**Strided footprint.** With stride S, the warp's 32 reads cover `32 * S * 4` bytes. The number of 128-byte segments touched is roughly `ceil(32*S*4 / 128) = ceil(S)` -- so stride 1 is 1 segment, stride 2 is 2 segments, stride 32 is 32 segments (one per thread). Efficiency = 128 useful bytes / (segments * 128) = 1/segments.

sections.title: Coalesced vs Strided Access
sections.content:
### Coalesced Access Pattern (Good) When consecutive threads access consecutive memory addresses, the accesses are **coalesced**: ```python @cuda.jit def coalesced_kernel(data, output): idx = cuda.grid(1) if idx < data.size: output[idx] = data[idx] * 2.0 # Thread 0 reads data[0], thread 1 reads data[1], ... ``` ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph mem["Memory: 128-byte segment (1 cache line)"] direction LR F0[float0] ~~~ F1[float1] ~~~ F2[float2] ~~~ Fe[...] ~~~ F31[float31] end subgraph warp["Warp: 32 threads"] direction LR T0[T0] ~~~ T1[T1] ~~~ T2[T2] ~~~ Te[...] ~~~ T31[T31] end T0 --> F0 T1 --> F1 T2 --> F2 T31 --> F31 Result["1 memory transaction
128 bytes fetched, 128 bytes used = 100%"] warp --> Result classDef ok fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 class Result ok ``` ### Strided Access Pattern (Bad) When threads access memory with a stride, coalescing breaks down: ```python @cuda.jit def strided_kernel(data, output, stride): idx = cuda.grid(1) if idx * stride < data.size: output[idx] = data[idx * stride] * 2.0 # Thread 0 reads data[0], thread 1 reads data[stride], ... ``` With stride=2: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph cl0["Cache line 0 (128B)"] direction TB L0["data[0..31] (used: 0,2,4,...,30)"] end subgraph cl1["Cache line 1 (128B)"] direction TB L1["data[32..63] (used: 32,34,...,62)"] end T0[T0..T15] --> cl0 T1[T16..T31] --> cl1 Note["2 cache lines = 2 transactions
256 bytes fetched, 128 bytes used = 50% efficient"] classDef warn fill:#78350f,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#fef3c7 class Note warn ``` With stride=32: ``` 32 cache lines needed = 32 memory transactions (4096 bytes fetched, 128 bytes used) Efficiency: 3.1% -- almost 97% of bandwidth is wasted! ``` > **Quick check:** Your kernel accesses `data[idx * 4]` where idx is the thread index. How many cache lines does one warp touch? What is the bandwidth efficiency? (Answer: stride=4 means 32 threads access addresses spread over 32*4*4 = 512 bytes. That is 4 cache lines of 128 bytes each. 128 bytes useful / 512 bytes fetched = 25% efficient.) ### The Row-Major Memory Layout Problem The stride problem becomes critical with 2D arrays. In row-major layout (which numpy and C use), a matrix is stored row by row in memory: ``` Matrix: Memory layout: M[0][0] M[0][1] M[0][2] M[0][0] M[0][1] M[0][2] M[1][0] M[1][1] M[1][2] ... M[1][0] M[1][1] M[1][2] ^ Contiguous in memory along rows ``` **Accessing elements along a row** (varying column, fixed row): **COALESCED** -- consecutive threads access consecutive memory addresses. ```python # GOOD: threads in warp access consecutive columns (same row) val = matrix[row, col] # Where col varies across threads # Thread 0: matrix[0, 0], Thread 1: matrix[0, 1], ... -> consecutive addresses ``` **Accessing elements along a column** (fixed column, varying row): **NOT COALESCED** -- each thread accesses a different row, separated by `N * sizeof(float)` bytes. ```python # BAD: threads in warp access consecutive rows (same column) val = matrix[row, col] # Where row varies across threads # Thread 0: matrix[0, 0], Thread 1: matrix[1, 0], ... -> strided by N floats! ```

sections.title: AoS vs SoA & Transpose
sections.content:
### Array of Structures (AoS) vs. Structure of Arrays (SoA)

This coalescing principle has profound implications for data layout. Consider representing particles with position (x, y, z) and velocity (vx, vy, vz):

**Array of Structures (AoS):** Each particle's data is grouped together.

```python
# AoS layout: [x0, y0, z0, vx0, vy0, vz0, x1, y1, z1, vx1, vy1, vz1, ...]
particles = np.zeros((N, 6), dtype=np.float32)  # Each row is one particle

@cuda.jit
def update_aos(particles):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < particles.shape[0]:
        particles[idx, 0] += particles[idx, 3]  # x += vx
        particles[idx, 1] += particles[idx, 4]  # y += vy
        particles[idx, 2] += particles[idx, 5]  # z += vz
```

Problem: Thread 0 accesses particles[0, 0] (x0), thread 1 accesses particles[1, 0] (x1). These are 6 floats apart = stride of 6. **Not coalesced.**

**Structure of Arrays (SoA):** Each property is stored in its own array.

```python
# SoA layout: x = [x0, x1, x2, ...], y = [y0, y1, y2, ...], ...
x = np.zeros(N, dtype=np.float32)
y = np.zeros(N, dtype=np.float32)
z = np.zeros(N, dtype=np.float32)
vx = np.zeros(N, dtype=np.float32)
vy = np.zeros(N, dtype=np.float32)
vz = np.zeros(N, dtype=np.float32)

@cuda.jit
def update_soa(x, y, z, vx, vy, vz):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < x.size:
        x[idx] += vx[idx]  # Thread 0: x[0], Thread 1: x[1] -> consecutive!
        y[idx] += vy[idx]
        z[idx] += vz[idx]
```

Now thread 0 accesses x[0], thread 1 accesses x[1] -- **perfectly coalesced.** The SoA version can be 2-5x faster than AoS on GPUs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph aos["AoS - Array of Structures (BAD: stride-6)"]
    direction LR
    A0["x0,y0,z0,vx0,vy0,vz0"] --- A1["x1,y1,z1,vx1,vy1,vz1"] --- A2["x2,y2,z2,vx2,vy2,vz2"]
    AT0[T0] -.reads x0.-> A0
    AT1[T1] -.reads x1.-> A1
    AT2[T2] -.reads x2.-> A2
  end
  subgraph soa["SoA - Structure of Arrays (GOOD: contiguous per field)"]
    direction TB
    XS["x array: x0, x1, x2, x3, x4, ..."]
    VS["vx array: vx0, vx1, vx2, vx3, ..."]
    ST0[T0,T1,T2,T3,T4] -.coalesced read.-> XS
    ST0 -.coalesced read.-> VS
  end
  Result["On T4 (2M particles): AoS ~0.35ms, SoA ~0.12ms ≈ 2.9x faster (estimated)"]
  classDef bad fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca
  classDef good fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0
  class A0,A1,A2 bad
  class XS,VS good
```

### Matrix Transpose: A Classic Coalescing Problem

Transposing a matrix is a classic case where naive implementation has terrible coalescing. Consider transposing matrix A into matrix B:

```python
# Naive transpose: uncoalesced writes
@cuda.jit
def naive_transpose(A, B):
    col, row = cuda.grid(2)
    if row < A.shape[0] and col < A.shape[1]:
        B[col, row] = A[row, col]  # Read: coalesced (along row)
                                    # Write: NOT coalesced (along column of B)
```

The read `A[row, col]` is coalesced because adjacent threads read adjacent columns of A. But the write `B[col, row]` is uncoalesced because adjacent threads write to adjacent rows of B (column-major pattern).

The solution uses shared memory as a staging area:

1. Load a tile of A into shared memory (coalesced read from global)
2. Read from shared memory with transposed indices (fast, bank conflict-free with padding)
3. Write to B (coalesced write to global)

```python
TILE_DIM = 32

@cuda.jit
def tiled_transpose(A, B):
    # +1 padding to avoid bank conflicts
    tile = cuda.shared.array((TILE_DIM, TILE_DIM + 1), dtype=float32)

    col, row = cuda.grid(2)
    tx = cuda.threadIdx.x
    ty = cuda.threadIdx.y

    # Coalesced read from A into shared memory
    if row < A.shape[0] and col < A.shape[1]:
        tile[ty, tx] = A[row, col]

    cuda.syncthreads()

    # Compute transposed output position
    # The key insight: blockIdx is swapped for the write
    new_col = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_DIM + tx
    new_row = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_DIM + ty

    # Coalesced write to B from shared memory (transposed)
    if new_row < B.shape[0] and new_col < B.shape[1]:
        B[new_row, new_col] = tile[tx, ty]  # Note: tx and ty swapped!
```

The `+ 1` padding in `(TILE_DIM, TILE_DIM + 1)` prevents bank conflicts when reading columns from shared memory. Without padding, all threads in a warp would access the same bank (stride-32 access on a 32-bank shared memory).
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda, float32

# ============================================
# Memory Coalescing: Stride Access Benchmark
# ============================================

@cuda.jit
def stride_access(data, output, stride):
    """Access data with a given stride between threads."""
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    data_idx = idx * stride
    if data_idx < data.size:
        output[idx] = data[data_idx]

@cuda.jit
def coalesced_access(data, output):
    """Access consecutive elements (stride=1, coalesced)."""
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        output[idx] = data[idx]

print("=== Coalescing: Stride vs Consecutive Access ===")
n = 10_000_000
data = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
d_data = cuda.to_device(data)

threads = 256
blocks = (n // 32 + threads - 1) // threads  # enough for stride-32

d_out = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)

# Warm up
coalesced_access[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()

iters = 100

for stride in [1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32]:
    n_elements = n // stride
    bl = (n_elements + threads - 1) // threads
    d_out_s = cuda.device_array(n_elements, dtype=np.float32)

    # Warm up
    stride_access[bl, threads](d_data, d_out_s, stride)
    cuda.synchronize()

    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        stride_access[bl, threads](d_data, d_out_s, stride)
    cuda.synchronize()
    t = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

    bytes_moved = n_elements * 4 * 2  # read + write
    bw = bytes_moved / t / 1e9
    print(f"  Stride {stride:>2}: {t*1000:.3f} ms, effective BW: {bw:.1f} GB/s")

# ============================================
# AoS vs SoA Demonstration
# ============================================
print("\n=== AoS vs SoA Layout ===")

N_PARTICLES = 2_000_000

# AoS: each particle = [x, y, z, vx, vy, vz]
aos = np.random.randn(N_PARTICLES, 6).astype(np.float32)
d_aos = cuda.to_device(aos)

# SoA: separate arrays for each property
x = np.random.randn(N_PARTICLES).astype(np.float32)
y = np.random.randn(N_PARTICLES).astype(np.float32)
z = np.random.randn(N_PARTICLES).astype(np.float32)
vx = np.random.randn(N_PARTICLES).astype(np.float32)
vy = np.random.randn(N_PARTICLES).astype(np.float32)
vz = np.random.randn(N_PARTICLES).astype(np.float32)
d_x, d_y, d_z = cuda.to_device(x), cuda.to_device(y), cuda.to_device(z)
d_vx, d_vy, d_vz = cuda.to_device(vx), cuda.to_device(vy), cuda.to_device(vz)

@cuda.jit
def update_aos(particles):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < particles.shape[0]:
        particles[idx, 0] += particles[idx, 3]  # x += vx (stride-6 access!)
        particles[idx, 1] += particles[idx, 4]  # y += vy
        particles[idx, 2] += particles[idx, 5]  # z += vz

@cuda.jit
def update_soa(x, y, z, vx, vy, vz):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < x.size:
        x[idx] += vx[idx]  # Coalesced!
        y[idx] += vy[idx]
        z[idx] += vz[idx]

blocks_p = (N_PARTICLES + threads - 1) // threads

# Warm up
update_aos[blocks_p, threads](d_aos)
update_soa[blocks_p, threads](d_x, d_y, d_z, d_vx, d_vy, d_vz)
cuda.synchronize()

# Benchmark AoS
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iters):
    update_aos[blocks_p, threads](d_aos)
cuda.synchronize()
aos_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

# Benchmark SoA
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iters):
    update_soa[blocks_p, threads](d_x, d_y, d_z, d_vx, d_vy, d_vz)
cuda.synchronize()
soa_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

print(f"  AoS (stride-6 access): {aos_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"  SoA (coalesced access): {soa_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"  SoA speedup: {aos_time/soa_time:.2f}x")
print(f"\n  SoA is faster because consecutive threads access")
print(f"  consecutive memory addresses (coalesced).")
print(f"  AoS forces stride-6 access, wasting memory bandwidth.")

# ============================================
# Matrix Transpose: Naive vs Tiled
# ============================================
print("\n=== Matrix Transpose: Naive vs Tiled ===")

TILE_DIM = 32

@cuda.jit
def naive_transpose(A, B):
    col, row = cuda.grid(2)
    if row < A.shape[0] and col < A.shape[1]:
        B[col, row] = A[row, col]

@cuda.jit
def tiled_transpose(A, B):
    tile = cuda.shared.array((TILE_DIM, TILE_DIM + 1), dtype=float32)  # +1 padding
    tx = cuda.threadIdx.x
    ty = cuda.threadIdx.y

    # Input coordinates
    row = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_DIM + ty
    col = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_DIM + tx

    # Coalesced read from A
    if row < A.shape[0] and col < A.shape[1]:
        tile[ty, tx] = A[row, col]

    cuda.syncthreads()

    # Output coordinates (swapped blocks)
    new_row = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_DIM + ty
    new_col = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_DIM + tx

    # Coalesced write to B
    if new_row < B.shape[0] and new_col < B.shape[1]:
        B[new_row, new_col] = tile[tx, ty]  # tx,ty swapped = transposed

M = 4096
A = np.random.randn(M, M).astype(np.float32)
d_A = cuda.to_device(A)
d_B_naive = cuda.device_array((M, M), dtype=np.float32)
d_B_tiled = cuda.device_array((M, M), dtype=np.float32)

# Note: (32, 32) = 1024 threads per block, which is the hardware maximum.
# This works for simple kernels but may fail if the kernel uses many registers.
# An alternative is (32, 8) with each thread processing multiple rows.
TPB_2d = (TILE_DIM, TILE_DIM)
BPG_2d = ((M + TILE_DIM - 1) // TILE_DIM, (M + TILE_DIM - 1) // TILE_DIM)

# Warm up
naive_transpose[BPG_2d, TPB_2d](d_A, d_B_naive)
tiled_transpose[BPG_2d, TPB_2d](d_A, d_B_tiled)
cuda.synchronize()

# Benchmark
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iters):
    naive_transpose[BPG_2d, TPB_2d](d_A, d_B_naive)
cuda.synchronize()
naive_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iters):
    tiled_transpose[BPG_2d, TPB_2d](d_A, d_B_tiled)
cuda.synchronize()
tiled_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

# Verify
assert np.allclose(d_B_naive.copy_to_host(), A.T, atol=1e-5)
assert np.allclose(d_B_tiled.copy_to_host(), A.T, atol=1e-5)

bytes_total = 2 * M * M * 4  # read + write
print(f"  Naive:  {naive_time*1000:.3f} ms ({bytes_total/naive_time/1e9:.0f} GB/s)")
print(f"  Tiled:  {tiled_time*1000:.3f} ms ({bytes_total/tiled_time/1e9:.0f} GB/s)")
print(f"  Speedup: {naive_time/tiled_time:.2f}x")

sections.title: Rules & Mental Model
sections.content:
### Performance Impact: Real Numbers

On a T4 GPU with a 4096x4096 float32 matrix (numbers are typical, not guaranteed -- run the benchmark in this module to see your hardware's exact figures):

| Approach | Time | Effective Bandwidth | % of Peak |
|----------|------|--------------------|-----------|
| Naive transpose | ~2.5 ms | ~53 GB/s | 17% |
| Tiled transpose (shared mem) | ~0.9 ms | ~150 GB/s | 47% |
| Optimal (cuBLAS) | ~0.7 ms | ~192 GB/s | 60% |
| Peak bandwidth (T4) | -- | 320 GB/s | 100% |

The tiled version achieves nearly 3x the effective bandwidth of the naive version, just by ensuring coalesced access patterns.

### Summary: Memory Coalescing Rules

1. **Consecutive threads should access consecutive memory addresses.** This is the golden rule.
2. **Use SoA layout instead of AoS** for GPU data structures.
3. **For 2D data, the fast-varying dimension (columns in row-major) should map to threadIdx.x.** This connects directly to the 2D indexing from Module 2 -- now you understand WHY (16, 16) blocks work better than (1, 256).
4. **When coalescing is impossible (e.g., column access), use shared memory as a staging area** to convert the access pattern.
5. **Pad shared memory arrays** (add +1 to one dimension) to avoid bank conflicts when accessing columns.
6. **Mind alignment.** A coalesced footprint that starts on a non-128-byte boundary still costs an extra transaction. Prefer indices that begin at block boundaries.

### Think Like a GPU: Memory Coalescing Edition

Imagine 32 threads in a warp as 32 workers standing at a warehouse loading dock. Global memory is a massive warehouse where items are stored on shelves.

**Coalesced access:** All 32 workers ask for items from the same shelf (adjacent addresses). The warehouse worker grabs the entire shelf in one trip and hands each person their item. One trip, everyone served.

**Strided access:** Each worker asks for an item from a different shelf on a different floor. The warehouse worker must make 32 separate trips, one to each floor. 32x slower.

Your job as a GPU programmer: arrange your data so that when 32 threads execute simultaneously, they always ask for items from the same shelf.

### What Would Happen If...

- **What if you transposed a matrix without shared memory?** Either the reads or the writes would be uncoalesced (column-wise access). On T4 with a 4096x4096 matrix: naive transpose achieves ~53 GB/s (17% of peak bandwidth). Shared memory tiled version: ~150 GB/s (47% of peak). That is a 2.8x speedup just from fixing the access pattern.

- **What if you stored RGB image data as AoS (R0,G0,B0,R1,G1,B1,...) vs SoA (R0,R1,R2...,G0,G1,G2...,B0,B1,B2...)?** Processing just the red channel with AoS means stride-3 access. With SoA, the red channel is contiguous. For image processing on T4: SoA can be 2-3x faster for channel-specific operations.

- **What if you did not pad shared memory in the tiled transpose?** Without the `+1` padding (`TILE_DIM x TILE_DIM` instead of `TILE_DIM x TILE_DIM+1`), reading columns from shared memory causes 32-way bank conflicts. Each column access takes 32 cycles instead of 1. On T4: unpadded tiled transpose achieves ~80 GB/s vs padded ~150 GB/s.

> **Gotcha:** Memory coalescing issues do not produce errors -- they produce SLOW code that gives CORRECT results. Your kernel works fine, it is just using 3-30% of available memory bandwidth. The only way to detect coalescing problems is profiling. Always benchmark your kernels against the theoretical peak bandwidth to check if you have a coalescing issue.

### Looking Ahead

You now understand the three pillars of GPU memory optimization: the memory hierarchy (registers > shared > global), shared memory with synchronization, and memory coalescing. In Module 4, we will combine ALL of these concepts in the ultimate test case: tiled matrix multiplication. You will see how loading tiles into shared memory, synchronizing threads, and ensuring coalesced access patterns work together to achieve near-peak GPU performance.

quiz.question: Which GPU memory type is fastest but smallest?
options: Global memory
Shared memory
Registers
Constant memory
quiz.explanation: Registers are the fastest memory on the GPU (~0 cycle access, same speed as computation) but are extremely limited. Each thread gets a small number of registers (up to ~255), and the total register file per SM is 65,536 x 32-bit registers shared among all resident threads.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of cuda.syncthreads()?
options: It synchronizes data between CPU and GPU
It ensures all threads in a block have reached the same point before any proceed, preventing race conditions on shared memory
It flushes the GPU cache to global memory
It synchronizes threads across different blocks
quiz.explanation: cuda.syncthreads() is a barrier synchronization within a block. All threads in the block must reach the syncthreads() call before any of them can proceed past it. This is essential when one thread reads shared memory that another thread wrote. It does NOT synchronize across blocks -- blocks execute independently.

quiz.question: Why can placing cuda.syncthreads() inside an if-statement cause a deadlock?
options: syncthreads cannot be called inside any conditional
If some threads in the block skip the if-branch, they never reach the barrier, so threads that did reach it wait forever
The if-statement causes warp divergence which disables synchronization
syncthreads only works in the main kernel function, not inside branches
quiz.explanation: syncthreads() requires ALL threads in the block to reach the barrier. If it is inside a conditional that some threads do not enter (e.g., threads with idx >= data.size), those threads never call syncthreads(), and the threads waiting at the barrier will wait forever -- a deadlock. Always place syncthreads() where all threads will reach it.

quiz.question: What is memory coalescing?
options: Combining CPU and GPU memory into a unified address space
The GPU hardware's ability to combine consecutive memory accesses from threads in a warp into a single efficient memory transaction
A software technique for compressing data in GPU memory
The process of defragmenting GPU memory
quiz.explanation: Memory coalescing occurs when consecutive threads in a warp access consecutive memory addresses. The GPU hardware combines these accesses into a single 128-byte cache line fetch, serving all 32 threads at once. Without coalescing, each thread's access may require a separate memory transaction, reducing effective bandwidth by up to 32x.

quiz.question: For a row-major 2D matrix on the GPU, which access pattern is coalesced?
options: Threads accessing consecutive rows in the same column: matrix[row, 0] where row varies across threads
Threads accessing consecutive columns in the same row: matrix[0, col] where col varies across threads
Threads accessing the diagonal: matrix[idx, idx]
All 2D access patterns are equally efficient
quiz.explanation: In row-major layout, consecutive columns in the same row are consecutive in memory. When threads access consecutive columns (col varies across threads in a warp), the addresses are consecutive and the access is coalesced. Accessing consecutive rows (different rows, same column) creates a stride equal to the matrix width, which is not coalesced.

quiz.question: What is a bank conflict in shared memory?
options: Two different blocks trying to access the same shared memory
Multiple threads in a warp accessing different addresses in the same shared memory bank, causing serialized access
Running out of shared memory in a block
A conflict between shared memory and global memory caches
quiz.explanation: Shared memory is divided into 32 banks. When multiple threads in a warp access different addresses that map to the same bank (bank = address/4 % 32), those accesses are serialized. A 2-way conflict means 2x slowdown, a 32-way conflict means 32x slowdown. Consecutive access (shared[tid]) has no conflicts. The exception: all threads reading the same address causes a broadcast, not a conflict.

quiz.question: Why is Structure of Arrays (SoA) generally better than Array of Structures (AoS) on GPUs?
options: SoA uses less total memory
SoA enables coalesced memory access because consecutive threads access consecutive memory addresses within each property array
SoA is easier to program
SoA avoids the need for shared memory
quiz.explanation: With AoS layout, consecutive threads access struct fields that are separated by the struct size (e.g., stride-6 for a 6-field struct). With SoA, each property is in its own contiguous array, so consecutive threads access consecutive addresses -- perfectly coalesced. This can yield 2-5x speedups on memory-bound kernels.

quiz.question: How does the tiled matrix transpose use shared memory to improve coalescing?
options: It caches the entire matrix in shared memory
It loads a tile from global memory with coalesced reads, then writes the transposed tile back with coalesced writes by swapping indices through shared memory
It avoids using global memory entirely
It uses shared memory to store the transpose lookup table
quiz.explanation: The naive transpose has coalesced reads but uncoalesced writes (or vice versa). The tiled version loads a tile into shared memory with coalesced reads from the source matrix, then writes the tile to the destination matrix with coalesced writes -- but using transposed indices from shared memory. Shared memory access is fast enough that the added step of going through shared memory is more than compensated by the improved global memory coalescing.

exercise.title: Shared Memory Matrix Transpose
exercise.description:
Implement a matrix transpose kernel using shared memory to achieve coalesced memory access, and compare it against a naive implementation.

**Requirements:**
1. Implement `naive_transpose(A, B)` that directly reads A[row, col] and writes B[col, row]
2. Implement `shared_transpose(A, B)` that:
   - Loads a TILE_DIM x TILE_DIM tile from A into shared memory (coalesced read)
   - Uses cuda.syncthreads() after the load
   - Writes from shared memory to B with transposed indices (coalesced write)
   - Uses +1 padding on shared memory to avoid bank conflicts
3. Test with a non-square matrix (e.g., 2048 x 4096) to catch row/col bugs
4. Verify both produce the correct transpose (compare with A.T)
5. Benchmark both versions and compute effective bandwidth
6. Calculate what percentage of the GPU's peak bandwidth each achieves

**Hints:**
- TILE_DIM = 32 is standard (matches warp size)
- Shared memory declaration: `cuda.shared.array((TILE_DIM, TILE_DIM + 1), dtype=float32)`
- The +1 eliminates bank conflicts when reading columns
- For the tiled version, swap blockIdx.x and blockIdx.y when computing output coordinates
- Effective bandwidth = 2 * M * N * 4 bytes / time (read + write)
- T4 peak bandwidth is ~320 GB/s
- Note: Using (32, 32) = 1024 threads per block is at the hardware maximum. This works for this simple kernel but may need adjustment for complex kernels that use more registers.

exercise.solution:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda, float32

TILE_DIM = 32

@cuda.jit
def naive_transpose(A, B):
    """Transpose A into B without shared memory."""
    col, row = cuda.grid(2)
    if row < A.shape[0] and col < A.shape[1]:
        B[col, row] = A[row, col]

@cuda.jit
def shared_transpose(A, B):
    """Transpose A into B using shared memory for coalesced access."""
    tile = cuda.shared.array((TILE_DIM, TILE_DIM + 1), dtype=float32)
    tx = cuda.threadIdx.x
    ty = cuda.threadIdx.y

    # Input coordinates
    row = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_DIM + ty
    col = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_DIM + tx

    # Coalesced read from A into shared memory
    if row < A.shape[0] and col < A.shape[1]:
        tile[ty, tx] = A[row, col]

    cuda.syncthreads()

    # Output coordinates: swap block indices
    new_row = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_DIM + ty
    new_col = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_DIM + tx

    # Coalesced write from shared memory to B (transposed)
    if new_row < B.shape[0] and new_col < B.shape[1]:
        B[new_row, new_col] = tile[tx, ty]  # tx, ty swapped

# Test with non-square matrix
M, N = 2048, 4096
print(f"Matrix: {M} x {N} (non-square to catch row/col bugs)")
A = np.random.randn(M, N).astype(np.float32)

d_A = cuda.to_device(A)
d_B_naive = cuda.device_array((N, M), dtype=np.float32)
d_B_shared = cuda.device_array((N, M), dtype=np.float32)

# Launch configuration
# (32, 32) = 1024 threads/block -- at the hardware maximum.
# This works for this simple kernel. For complex kernels, use (32, 8)
# and have each thread process 4 rows.
TPB = (TILE_DIM, TILE_DIM)
BPG = ((N + TILE_DIM - 1) // TILE_DIM, (M + TILE_DIM - 1) // TILE_DIM)
print(f"Grid: {BPG}, Threads/block: {TPB}")

# Warm up
naive_transpose[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B_naive)
shared_transpose[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B_shared)
cuda.synchronize()

# Verify correctness
assert np.allclose(d_B_naive.copy_to_host(), A.T, atol=1e-5), "Naive transpose failed!"
assert np.allclose(d_B_shared.copy_to_host(), A.T, atol=1e-5), "Shared transpose failed!"
print("Both transposes verified correct!")

# Benchmark
iters = 100
bytes_total = 2 * M * N * 4  # read + write
peak_bw = 320  # T4 peak in GB/s

start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iters):
    naive_transpose[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B_naive)
cuda.synchronize()
naive_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters
naive_bw = bytes_total / naive_time / 1e9

start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iters):
    shared_transpose[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B_shared)
cuda.synchronize()
shared_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters
shared_bw = bytes_total / shared_time / 1e9

print(f"\n{'Method':<20} {'Time (ms)':<12} {'BW (GB/s)':<12} {'% Peak':<10} {'Speedup':<10}")
print("-" * 64)
print(f"{'Naive':<20} {naive_time*1000:<12.3f} {naive_bw:<12.1f} {naive_bw/peak_bw*100:<10.1f} {'1.00x':<10}")
print(f"{'Shared Memory':<20} {shared_time*1000:<12.3f} {shared_bw:<12.1f} {shared_bw/peak_bw*100:<10.1f} {naive_time/shared_time:<10.2f}x")
print(f"\nShared memory transpose achieves higher effective bandwidth by")
print(f"ensuring both reads and writes to global memory are coalesced.")
